notes: The studio's first stick (Oneironautics, 1988) · the founding text of 'slow.' Seven consecutive mornings, 5:47 to the horns · a boy, a dog, a harbor. Amélie Rocha's second credit (junior · the fish-cleaning station dialogue and one poster). Chapter 7's half-torn 1976 carnival poster plants Fey Faire two years before it ships.

shelf.label_subtitle: seven mornings · a boy, a dog, a harbor
shelf.shelf_condition: camp-worn · CAMP SWEETGUM stenciled on the sleeve in white paint · a checkout grid on the back, filled in pencil by dozens of hands
shelf.prior_owner: Camp Sweetgum's rec-hall console lot · Olaf, at auction, 2011
shelf.provenance_note: This is the camp cart itself. Olaf bought the Sweetgum console lot at auction in 2011 and this stick was in the box. Sam's 1988 save of Chapter One is still on it · mid-pocket, holding the glove. Olaf never deleted it. Tem has examined that save more than any other object on the shelf.
shelf.cover_blurb: The first game from Oneironautics Inc., a new studio in Portland, Oregon. Seven mornings in a harbor town. One boy, one dog, twelve places, and everything anybody says out loud. There is nothing to win. Bring a coat.
back_of_case: · The dog knows. Watch the dog.
'a game you cannot be good at, only present for.'
shelf.in_fiction_dev_note: The founding text. Marc Ostrom's first paid work is the eight-bar concertina over the chapter cards. Amélie Rocha, 23, junior, wrote the fish-cleaning station dialogue · and put a half-torn 1976 carnival poster on the cannery wall in Chapter 7. Nobody at the studio asked her why. The doc's answer: it was the only place she could put it.
shelf.meta_note: The camp copy's Chapter 2 chart has a stretch of coast blacked out · square, deliberate, done with a ruler. Someone at Oneironautics did it before the cart shipped to Sweetgum. The older maps call that stretch the DELISLE place.

lore_tokens_conditional.nh_good_week: every chain closed by the seventh horn · THE GOOD WEEK
lore_tokens_conditional.nh_bosun_ritual: petted Bosun six of seven mornings · the ritual he never asked for
